content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Corporate history is often littered with examples of good incentives gone wrong. The carrots must be achievable, but never effortless. The sticks should loom far away, but not forgettable. The KPIs should be everywhere, visible, measurable, and constantly judging you.
The challenge, unfortunately, lies in the fact that the true measures of productivity, engagement, skills, and so on are invisible. And everything that’s visible and measurable can be manipulated as well.
And that’s how the trouble began….through Jensen Huang of Nvidia.
In early 2026, the company’s CEO laid out an interesting thought in an interview: take a software engineer on a $500,000 salary. If that engineer’s annual AI token spend came in under $250,000, he said, he’d be deeply alarmed — the same way he’d be alarmed by a chip designer who insisted on working with pencil and paper instead of the firm’s design software.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Jensen Huang has become the celebrity godfather of the tech industry (and it’s not without merit). So it’s only natural that all the techies and leaders out there sat up and listened to what he had to say. And implemented it very intelligently. Within a span of hours, token spend became the proxy for productivity. Everyone was tracking it — employees, HR, and leaders alike. Almost everyone began maximising AI token usage and treating high volume as a badge of innovation. Within weeks, a new term was coined: tokenmaxxing.
It took a lot longer for the finance teams to catch up to the fact that their AI budget was overshooting. They hadn’t forecasted tokenmaxxing into their numbers. Some organizations ate up their annual budget in a matter of 4 months. And their employees have never worked harder.
It’s a clean example of misaligned incentives hiding inside what looked, on the surface, like a reasonable ask.
It’s also an instantly recognisable shape if you’ve spent any time in performance management. Someone has just designed a target around the thing that’s cheapest to log, not the thing that’s actually true.
The lines-of-code problem, rebooted
This had happened before. In the early 2000s, plenty of engineering orgs measured productivity in lines of code shipped. The engineers figured out that a bloated, repetitive solution scored better than an elegant one, and the metric quietly stopped meaning anything. Experts call it Goodhart’s Law in the workplace: once a measure becomes the target, it stops being a good measure, because people optimise for the number, not the thing the number was supposed to represent.
Tokenmaxxing is the same pattern, dressed up for the AI era. What makes it worse is that optimisation through tokenmaxxing isn’t just wasted effort. It’s wasted money, and time, and a lot of it.
Nudging Gone Wrong
Amazon built an internal leaderboard to track AI usage across engineering teams, encouraging staff to “tokenmaxx” as a way of demonstrating they were leaning into the tools. Employees did exactly what any behavioural economics in HR framework would have predicted: they started burning tokens on trivial, meaningless tasks purely to climb the rankings, because climbing the rankings was the thing being rewarded. They eventually took the leaderboard down.
Meta too ran something similar internally. In both cases, the intention was to nudge adoption. What actually got nudged was the appearance of adoption.
This is the part incentive structure design keeps forgetting: people don’t optimise for what you meant. They optimise for what you measure. Reward token spend and you’ll get token spend — divorced, if necessary, from anything resembling a shipped feature or a solved problem.
The bill arrives
The predictable consequence of rewarding the wrong behaviour is that the wrong behaviour scales up, and creeps up in the finances. Uber’s CTO reportedly burned through the company’s entire 2026 AI coding budget in about four months. Microsoft’s engineering teams reportedly exhausted their AI budget on a similar timeline. A widely cited MIT study found that AI-driven automation was actually the cheaper option in only a minority of the roles it examined — for most roles, once you account for implementation, oversight, and correction, humans remained the better economic bet, reviving the AI vs human labor cost question in boardrooms that had assumed it was already settled. Surveys of employers who’d already cut staff in favour of AI have found a striking share now regret it, and functions like customer service are seeing early signs of rehiring.
Naturally it doesnt mean that the new technology isn’t useful. It just shows without the right frameworks, it might as well be.
The nudge-theory read
This is worth sitting with, because it’s the same failure mode I keep circling back to in this blog, and will continue to do so. A completion rate is not the same as learning. A token count is not the same as productivity. Whenever an organisation reaches for the number that’s easiest to capture on a dashboard instead of the outcome that’s actually hard to observe, it’s practicing employee incentive design badly. It is enabling a structure that people will, rationally and predictably, game. Not because people are dishonest, but because that’s what incentives are for. They tell you what actually gets rewarded, and everyone adjusts accordingly.
The uncomfortable part for HR and people functions specifically: this pattern will keep showing up as more of our own performance metrics get automated and quantified. If the next generation of workforce analytics defaults to “what’s easy to log” over “what’s actually true,” we are literally incentivising the wrong behaviour. And worse, it will be too late before the error is realised.
The design principle, again
Before adopting any new usage-based metric as a proxy for productivity or engagement, ask what the cheapest possible way to inflate that number would look like — and whether anyone would actually want that outcome if they got it. If the answer is “no, but it would look great on the dashboard,” you haven’t designed an incentive. You’ve designed a very expensive loophole

If your org has quietly rolled out a usage leaderboard of its own, I’d genuinely like to hear how long it took someone to break it.
